messages.content: What personal data does Red Hat collect?

Red Hat collects contact information (name, email, phone, address), account and commercial information (account number, username, payment info, purchase records), professional information (job applications), internet activity (IP address, browsing history, browser type), location information, and inferences drawn from your data.
How does Red Hat collect my personal data?

Red Hat collects personal data directly from you when you create an account, make purchases, request support, register for events, participate in surveys, or apply for employment. Red Hat also collects data indirectly through website interactions, cookies, and from third parties like your employer or business partners.
What does Red Hat use my personal data for?

Red Hat uses your personal data to identify and authenticate you, fulfill your requests, provide information about products and services, offer support and customer service, enhance website experience, and process job applications. Red Hat may also use AI and machine learning to analyze data and provide AI-generated responses.
Does Red Hat share my personal data with third parties?

Yes, Red Hat discloses personal data to business partners and service providers (distributors, payment processors, shipping companies), as required by law or legal process, in business transactions (mergers, acquisitions), and to affiliates including IBM. Business partners are required by contract to keep information confidential and secure.
What types of cookies does Red Hat use?

Red Hat uses three categories of cookies: Required cookies (necessary for website functionality, authentication, security), Functional cookies (analyze website use, personalize experience, remember preferences), and Advertising cookies (show relevant ads, remarketing on other websites). You can manage cookie preferences through browser settings or Red Hat's cookie manager.
What are my privacy rights with Red Hat?

Depending on applicable law, you may have rights to request information about data use, access your personal data, rectify incorrect data, erase your data, restrict data use, object to certain processing, request data portability, and lodge complaints with data protection authorities. Exercise these rights via Red Hat's Personal Data Request Form.
How can I contact Red Hat about privacy concerns?

Contact Red Hat about privacy at privacy@redhat.com, via the Personal Data Request Form at redhat.com/en/about/personal-data-request, or by mail at Red Hat, Inc., Corporate Legal Group, 100 East Davie Street, Raleigh, North Carolina 27601, United States. California consumers can call 1-800-546-7274.
Does Red Hat sell my personal data?

Red Hat does not sell personal data as commonly understood. However, under certain Data Protection Laws, disclosures to advertising partners who collect cookies for their own purposes may qualify as a 'sale.' You can opt out of such uses through cookie preferences or browser settings.
Where is my Red Hat personal data stored?

Red Hat is a global organization and may transfer your personal data to Red Hat entities in the United States and elsewhere. For EEA, UK, or Switzerland residents, Red Hat has certified to the EU-U.S. and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Frameworks. Appropriate safeguards are in place for international transfers.
How long does Red Hat keep my personal data?

Red Hat retains personal data only as long as required to fulfill processing purposes or for other valid reasons such as legal and regulatory obligations, dispute resolution, enforcing agreements, or establishing legal claims. The retention period depends on the specific purpose and applicable legal requirements.
Can I opt out of Red Hat marketing emails?

Yes, you can opt out of marketing emails at any time by clicking the unsubscribe link in marketing emails or contacting Red Hat via the Personal Data Request Form. You can also set preferences when creating an account or registering for services.
What is Red Hat's Data Privacy Framework certification?

Red Hat, Inc. and Red Hat Professional Consulting, Inc. have certified to the EU-U.S. and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Frameworks for transferring personal data from the EEA and Switzerland to the United States, as well as the UK Extension. This provides a legal basis for international data transfers.
Does Red Hat collect data from children?

Red Hat's products and services are not directed to children, and Red Hat does not knowingly collect online personal data from children under 16. If you believe a child under 16 has disclosed personal data, contact Red Hat via the Personal Data Request Form, and Red Hat will properly process and dispose of the data with parental consent.
What information does Red Hat collect through telemetry?

Red Hat collects telemetry data including software implementation size (cluster size, CPU cores, RAM), software version and updates, component and feature usage, update errors and progress, unique random identifiers, operating system and tool information, configuration details, and information about degraded software. You can enable or disable telemetry upon first use.
How does Red Hat protect my personal data?

Red Hat implements physical, administrative, and technical safeguards to protect personal data from unauthorized access, use, and disclosure. This includes encrypting certain data like payment information during transmission and requiring business partners to protect information through contractual obligations.
Can I access my Red Hat account data online?

Yes, you can log in and modify some personal data online, such as password, contact information, general preferences, and personalization settings. For other data requests or modifications, use the Personal Data Request Form. Note that changing essential information may delay processing of support or purchase requests.
What is Red Hat's lead supervisory authority in the EEA?

Red Hat's lead supervisory authority in the European Economic Area is the Irish Data Protection Commissioner. EEA residents can direct privacy questions or complaints to the Office of the Data Protection Commissioner at Canal House, Station Road, Portarlington, Co. Laois, R32 AP23, Ireland, or contact their local supervisory authority.
Does Red Hat use AI to process my data?

Yes, Red Hat may use artificial intelligence and machine learning capabilities, including those from third-party service providers, to analyze data, identify trends, make predictions, and provide AI-generated responses. This is used to facilitate the purposes described in the privacy statement, such as enhancing website experience and providing support.
What happens to my data if Red Hat is acquired?

In business transactions like mergers, acquisitions, or divestitures, Red Hat may disclose personal data to prospective or actual purchasers. Red Hat seeks appropriate protection for information in such transactions and will inform you of the name and contact information of the receiver of your personal data.
Can I request a copy of my Red Hat personal data?

Yes, you have the right to request information about Red Hat's use of your personal data, including the pieces and categories of data collected, sources, purposes, and third parties to whom data has been disclosed. Submit requests via the Personal Data Request Form at redhat.com/en/about/personal-data-request.
What is Red Hat's relationship with IBM regarding data?

Red Hat may disclose personal data to its parent company, International Business Machines Corporation (IBM), and other affiliates for business purposes described in the privacy statement. Data transfers to IBM are conducted in accordance with the Data transfers and Data Privacy Frameworks section of the privacy statement.
How often does Red Hat update its privacy statement?

Red Hat reserves the right to update the Privacy Statement at any time. When changed in a material way, a notice is posted for 30 days and the last update date is indicated at the top. The current version was last updated October 13, 2025. Previous versions are archived and available upon request.
What data does Red Hat collect when I visit redhat.com?

When you visit redhat.com, Red Hat automatically collects internet activity information including IP address, browsing history, website interaction data, browser type and language, operating system, location, date and time. This information may be combined with data you provide offline, such as through call centers or events.
Can I delete my Red Hat account data?

Yes, you have the right to request erasure (deletion) of your personal data, subject to limitations under applicable law. However, Red Hat may retain data for valid reasons like legal obligations, dispute resolution, or enforcing agreements. Submit deletion requests via the Personal Data Request Form.
What are Red Hat's required cookies used for?

Red Hat's required cookies are necessary for basic website functionality, including maintaining security, authenticating users, allowing access to private accounts, enabling single sign-on across website portions, balancing website traffic, and remembering shopping cart items. Required cookies are not used for marketing and cannot be turned off.
Does Red Hat collect sensitive personal data?

Red Hat generally does not collect sensitive personal data such as religious beliefs, racial origin, health information (except for event accommodation requests), genetic or biometric data, or bank account information (except for processing orders). If sensitive data collection is needed, Red Hat will obtain consent as required by applicable law.
How can I verify my identity when requesting privacy rights?

Red Hat requires verification before responding to privacy requests. Initially, Red Hat asks for at least two or three identifiers such as name, email address, and location. Additional verification may be requested depending on your relationship with Red Hat. Authorized agents must provide written permission and verify identity.
What is Red Hat's address for privacy inquiries?

For privacy inquiries, contact Red Hat, Inc., Corporate Legal Group, 100 East Davie Street, Raleigh, North Carolina 27601, United States. For EEA inquiries, contact Red Hat Limited, 6700 Cork Airport Business Park, Phase II 1st Floor, Cork, Ireland. You can also email privacy@redhat.com or use the Personal Data Request Form.
Does Red Hat use web beacons?

Yes, Red Hat uses web beacons alone or with cookies to compile information about website usage and email interactions (open rates, click-through rates). Web beacons are clear electronic images that recognize certain information on your device, such as cookies, when you view websites tied to the beacon.
Can I withdraw consent for Red Hat data processing?

Yes, where applicable Data Protection Laws apply, you have the right to withdraw consent previously given for data processing. Submit withdrawal requests via the Personal Data Request Form. However, withdrawal does not affect the lawfulness of processing based on consent before withdrawal.
What data does Red Hat collect during job applications?

Red Hat collects professional and employment-related information during recruitment, application, and hiring processes, including information contained in job inquiries or applications. For detailed information about candidate data processing, see the Red Hat Candidate Privacy Notice at redhat.com/en/about/candidate-privacy-notice.
Does Red Hat share data with social media platforms?

Red Hat provides social media features that enable sharing information with social networks. Your use of these features may result in collection or sharing of information about you. Red Hat encourages you to review privacy policies and settings on social media websites you interact with to understand what information may be collected, used, and shared.
What is the scope of Red Hat's privacy statement?

This Privacy Statement applies to personal data collected through redhat.com and other websites where Red Hat posts a direct link to this statement. It may also apply when Red Hat makes the statement available in digital communications, paper forms, or in person. It does not apply to open source project websites or co-branded websites with different privacy statements.
How does Red Hat handle data in cloud services like OpenShift Online?

Red Hat's obligations regarding personal data held on behalf of customers in cloud services like OpenShift Online are defined in agreements with customers and are not governed by this Privacy Statement. This statement applies to Red Hat's collection of data, not data customers store using Red Hat cloud services.
What are functional cookies used for on Red Hat websites?

Functional cookies allow Red Hat to analyze website use, evaluate performance, personalize browsing experience, remember language and site preferences, invite participation in surveys, maintain login sessions, provide support services, prepopulate forms, measure website traffic, test designs, and understand user interests to improve website quality and advertising effectiveness.
Can I appeal if Red Hat denies my privacy rights request?

Yes, where applicable Data Protection Laws provide a right to appeal, and Red Hat informs you it cannot take action on your request, you may appeal the decision within a reasonable period. Submit appeals by selecting 'Other' via the Personal Data Request Form or by emailing privacy@redhat.com.
What information does Red Hat collect from third parties?

Red Hat may supplement data collected from you with additional personal data from third parties such as your employer, customers, and business partners when you purchase products or services through them. This includes account and commercial information and professional information, used to improve accuracy, tailor interactions, and support marketing and sales efforts.
Does Red Hat discriminate if I exercise privacy rights?

No, when you exercise privacy rights under applicable Data Protection Laws, you have a right not to receive discriminatory treatment from Red Hat for exercising such rights. Red Hat will not penalize or treat you differently for requesting information, accessing data, or exercising other privacy rights.
What is personal data according to Red Hat's privacy statement?

According to Red Hat's Privacy Statement, 'personal data' means any information that relates to, is capable of being associated with, describes, or could be linked to an identified or identifiable natural person. An identifiable person can be identified directly or indirectly by reference to identifiers like name, identification number, location data, or online identifier.
How does Red Hat use my data for marketing?

Subject to your preferences, Red Hat may use your personal data to notify you about product and service offerings and events, personalize online experience with content and advertisements, develop marketing and business intelligence, measure marketing campaign effectiveness, develop marketing audiences, assist in prioritization, and tailor communication efforts.
What happens if I don't accept Red Hat's privacy statement changes?

If Red Hat makes material changes to the Privacy Statement, a notice is posted for 30 days. If you do not refuse the changes in writing within that period and continue using Red Hat websites, Red Hat considers that you have read and understand the Privacy Statement as changed, including with respect to data provided before the changes.
Does Red Hat collect location data?

Yes, Red Hat collects location information about your physical location, such as if you provide your postal address or based on your IP address when you visit a website. Location information is used for purposes like personalizing experience, providing relevant content, and supporting business operations.
What are advertising cookies used for on Red Hat websites?

Advertising cookies are used to show ads more relevant to you. Red Hat may disclose this information to advertisers or use it to better understand your interests. Advertising cookies may be used for remarketing technology to deliver targeted Red Hat advertisements on other websites you visit, with third parties placing ad-serving cookies on your device.
How does Red Hat handle data from list vendors?

Red Hat may collect contact information from list vendors. When doing so, Red Hat asks vendors to confirm that the information was legally acquired and that Red Hat has the right to obtain it and use it. This helps ensure compliance with data protection requirements when using third-party data sources.
What is Red Hat's data retention policy?

Red Hat retains personal data only as long as required to fulfill processing purposes or for other valid reasons including legal and regulatory obligations, dispute resolution, enforcing agreements, and establishing, exercising, or defending legal claims. Retention periods vary based on the specific purpose and applicable legal requirements.
Can I request data portability from Red Hat?

Yes, you have the right to receive your personal data in a usable format and transmit it to a third party (data portability), subject to applicable Data Protection Laws. You also have the right to request transfer of your personal data to a third party. Submit these requests via the Personal Data Request Form.
What safeguards does Red Hat have for international data transfers?

Red Hat has put in place appropriate safeguards such as standard contractual clauses approved by the European Commission, in accordance with applicable legal requirements, to ensure data is adequately protected during international transfers. Red Hat protects personal data as provided in the Privacy Statement and complies with applicable legal requirements.
Does Red Hat use my data for customer satisfaction surveys?

Yes, Red Hat or its representatives may contact you as part of customer satisfaction surveys or for market research purposes. This is part of Red Hat's use of personal data to fulfill requests and improve services. Survey participation is typically voluntary, and you can opt out of marketing communications.
What information does Red Hat collect during support interactions?

Red Hat collects personal data including name, title, company name, email, phone, address, account number, username, payment information, purchase records, product usage information, event registration data, preferences, credit and billing status, and support inquiries. In technical support, Red Hat may have incidental access to data on your systems, governed by your agreement with Red Hat, not this Privacy Statement.
How can I manage Red Hat cookie preferences?

You can manage cookie preferences through your web browser settings (enable, disable, or delete cookies) or through Red Hat's cookie manager if implemented on the website (click 'Cookie Preferences' or similar link at the bottom). Note that Required cookies cannot be turned off, and rejecting cookies may reduce website performance and functionality.
What is Red Hat's policy on Do Not Track signals?

If you have set your browser's global privacy settings such as selecting 'Do Not Track,' Red Hat recognizes these settings to signal your choice to opt-out of 'sales' and targeted advertising as appropriate under applicable Data Protection Laws. Red Hat respects browser privacy settings for tracking preferences.
Does Red Hat collect data from public forums on its websites?

Red Hat websites may make chat rooms, forums, blogs, message boards, and news groups available. Remember that your comments and posts become publicly available. Red Hat encourages you to exercise discretion when submitting content, as this Privacy Statement applies to information you provide in these public spaces.
What is Red Hat's relationship with IBM regarding data privacy?

Red Hat may disclose personal data to its parent company, International Business Machines Corporation (IBM), and other affiliates for business purposes. Data transfers to IBM are conducted in accordance with the Data transfers and Data Privacy Frameworks section, with appropriate safeguards for international transfers.
Can I get a PDF version of Red Hat's privacy statement?

Yes, a PDF version of the Privacy Statement is available at redhat.com/rhdc/managed-files/RH-Online-Privacy-Statement_English_13-Oct-2025.pdf.pdf. The current version was last updated October 13, 2025, and previous versions are archived and available upon request.
What is Red Hat's definition of personal data?

Red Hat defines personal data as any information that relates to, is capable of being associated with, describes, or could be linked to an identified or identifiable natural person. An identifiable person can be identified directly or indirectly by reference to identifiers like name, identification number, location data, online identifier, or factors specific to physical, physiological, genetic, mental, economic, cultural, or social identity.
What contact information does Red Hat collect?

Red Hat collects contact information including first and last name, title, company name, email address, telephone number, and postal address. This information is used to communicate with you and verify your identity.
What account information does Red Hat maintain?

Red Hat maintains account information such as account number, username/user ID, password, payment information, purchase records, product or service usage information, registration or participation in classes, exams, certifications, training, webcasts or events, preferred language and other preferences, credit and billing status, and support inquiries.
What professional information does Red Hat collect during hiring?

Red Hat collects professional or employment-related information during recruitment, application, and hiring processes, including information contained in job inquiries or applications. For detailed information, see the Red Hat Candidate Privacy Notice.
What internet activity data does Red Hat collect?

Red Hat collects internet activity information when you visit websites, including IP address, browsing history, information on your interaction with the website, browser type and language, operating system, location, date and time.
What inferences does Red Hat draw from my data?

Red Hat draws inferences from collected personal data, such as what topics you may be interested in based on website areas you visit, or what products you may be interested in based on purchase records. These inferences help personalize your experience and tailor communications.
When does Red Hat collect data directly from me?

Red Hat collects data directly from you when you create a user account (individual or corporate), make online purchases or register products, request support, register for classes/exams/certifications/training/webcasts/events, request information or materials, participate in surveys or evaluations, participate in promotions/contests/giveaways, apply for employment, submit questions or comments (including via chatbots), or submit content or posts on customer portal pages or interactive webpages.
How does Red Hat combine online and offline data?

Personal data collected online may be combined with personal data you provide through offline channels such as through a call center, during an interview, or in conjunction with a Red Hat event you attend. This helps Red Hat maintain a complete picture of your interactions.
What third-party data sources does Red Hat use?

Red Hat may supplement data with additional personal data from third parties such as your employer, customers, and business partners when you purchase products or services through them. Red Hat may also collect contact information from list vendors, asking vendors to confirm the information was legally acquired.
How does Red Hat use data for identity authentication?

Red Hat uses personal data to verify your identity when accessing and using services and to ensure security of your personal data. This includes creating an account associated with your personal data. Data used includes name, title, company name, email, phone, address, account number, username/user ID, password, preferred language and preferences.
What data does Red Hat use to fulfill requests?

Red Hat uses personal data including name, title, company name, email, phone, address, account number, username/user ID, payment information, purchase records, product or service usage, event registration, preferred language and preferences, credit and billing status, and support inquiries to fulfill requests for products, services, callbacks, newsletter subscriptions, or marketing materials.
How does Red Hat personalize my online experience?

Red Hat uses your personal data to personalize your online experience with content and advertisements, deliver content and product offerings relevant to your interests, provide targeted offers through websites, third-party sites or email (with consent where required), and help improve and develop websites, products and services.
What data does Red Hat access during technical support?

In technical support, Red Hat may have incidental access to data you provided or data located on your systems, which may contain information about you or your organization's business, employees, customers, partners, or suppliers. This data handling is governed by your agreement with Red Hat, not the Privacy Statement.
How does Red Hat use data for website enhancement?

Red Hat uses personal data to enhance and customize your website experience, deliver relevant content and product offerings, help improve and develop websites, products and services. This includes using data about website use, interactions with emails, and inferences drawn from your behavior.
What business partners receive my Red Hat data?

Red Hat discloses personal data to business partners and service providers including distributors, resellers, payment processors, financial service providers, materials production and shipping companies, postal or government authorities, market intelligence and consulting service providers, and information technology service providers.
What are the requirements for Red Hat's business partners?

Business partners and service providers are required by contract to keep information received on behalf of Red Hat confidential and secure and not use it for any purpose other than the purpose for which it was provided to them.
When does Red Hat disclose data as required by law?

Red Hat may disclose personal data as required by law or legal process, such as responding to duly authorized and lawful requests of police or public authorities (including national security or law enforcement), to enforce or protect Red Hat's rights, when disclosure is necessary to prevent physical harm or financial loss, or in connection with investigations of suspected or actual illegal activity.
What happens to my data in a Red Hat merger or acquisition?

In business transactions like mergers, acquisitions, consolidations, or divestitures, Red Hat may disclose personal data to prospective or actual purchasers or receive it from sellers. Red Hat seeks appropriate protection for information in these transactions and will inform you of the name and contact information of the receiver of your personal data.
What categories of data does Red Hat disclose to IBM?

Red Hat may disclose to IBM and affiliates the same categories of personal data collected: contact information including identifiers, account and commercial information, professional or employment-related information, internet or other similar network activity, location information, and inferences.
What is the difference between required and functional cookies?

Required cookies are necessary for basic website features to function and cannot be turned off. Functional cookies allow Red Hat to analyze website use, evaluate performance, personalize experience, and improve services. Both are used for website operations, but functional cookies provide analytics and personalization capabilities.
How do I disable Red Hat advertising cookies?

You can disable advertising cookies through your web browser settings (enable, disable, or delete cookies) or through Red Hat's cookie manager if available on the website (click 'Cookie Preferences' or similar link). You can also set your browser's global privacy settings like 'Do Not Track' to opt-out of targeted advertising.
What are session-based cookies?

Session-based cookies exist during a single session and disappear when you close your browser or turn off your device. They are temporary cookies used for functions like maintaining your login session during a single browsing session.
What are persistent cookies?

Persistent cookies remain on your device even after you close your browser or turn off your device. They are used to remember your preferences, maintain login sessions over time, and track your interactions across multiple visits to Red Hat websites.
How does Red Hat use web beacons in emails?

Red Hat places web beacons in marketing emails that notify Red Hat when you click on a link in the email that directs you to a Red Hat website. Red Hat uses web beacons to operate and improve websites and email communications, tracking open rates and click-through rates.
What information can I access in my Red Hat account?

You can access and modify online: your password, contact information, general preferences, and personalization settings. For other personal data requests, use the Personal Data Request Form. Note that changing essential information may delay processing of support or purchase requests.
How do I request information about my Red Hat data use?

You can request information about Red Hat's use of your personal data, including the pieces and categories of data collected, categories of sources, purposes for collection, third parties to whom data has been disclosed, and personal data that has been disclosed. Submit requests via the Personal Data Request Form at redhat.com/en/about/personal-data-request.
How do I correct incorrect Red Hat account information?

You have the right to rectify (correct, update, or modify) personal data Red Hat holds about you. You can modify some information online through your account, or contact Red Hat via the Personal Data Request Form to describe changes you want made to previously provided information.
Can I restrict how Red Hat uses my data?

Yes, you have the right to restrict Red Hat's use of your personal data, subject to limitations under applicable Data Protection Laws. Submit restriction requests via the Personal Data Request Form. Red Hat will evaluate your request in accordance with applicable legal requirements.
Can I object to Red Hat's data processing?

Yes, you have the right to object to Red Hat's use of, or certain types of disclosures of, your personal data, subject to limitations under applicable Data Protection Laws. Submit objections via the Personal Data Request Form, and Red Hat will evaluate your request in accordance with applicable legal requirements.
How do I lodge a complaint about Red Hat's data handling?

You have the right to lodge a complaint with your local data protection authority. For EEA residents, you can direct questions or complaints to the Irish Data Protection Commissioner (Red Hat's lead supervisory authority) or contact your local supervisory authority. Contact information is provided in the Privacy Statement.
What identifiers does Red Hat require for verification?

Red Hat initially asks for at least two or three identifiers to verify your identity, such as name, email address, and location. If additional verification is needed, Red Hat will contact you and request additional identifiers. The information requested may depend on your relationship with Red Hat.
Can someone else make a privacy request on my behalf?

Yes, you can designate an authorized agent or representative to make a request on your behalf by providing written permission or Power of Attorney. The authorized agent must provide name, email address, description of relationship, certify they have permission, and may need to provide proof of written permission.
What security measures does Red Hat implement?

Red Hat implements appropriate physical, administrative, and technical safeguards to protect personal data from unauthorized access, use, and disclosure. This includes encrypting certain data like payment information during transmission and requiring business partners and service providers to protect information from unauthorized access.
What should I know about Red Hat social media features?

Red Hat provides social media features that enable sharing information with social networks and interacting with Red Hat on social media websites. Your use of these features may result in collection or sharing of information about you. Red Hat encourages you to review privacy policies and settings on social media websites you interact with.
Are Red Hat forums and chat rooms public?

Yes, Red Hat websites may make chat rooms, forums, blogs, message boards, and news groups available. Your comments and posts become publicly available. Red Hat encourages you to exercise discretion when submitting content, as this Privacy Statement applies to information you provide in these public spaces.
Does Red Hat's privacy statement apply to linked websites?

No, Red Hat websites may contain links to other websites, but Red Hat does not control and is not responsible for information collected by websites reached through links from Red Hat websites. If you have questions about data collection procedures of linked websites, contact those organizations directly.
What is Red Hat's policy on children's data?

Red Hat's products and services are not directed to children, and Red Hat does not knowingly collect online personal data from children under 16. If you are a parent or guardian of a minor under 16 and believe they have disclosed personal data, contact Red Hat via the Personal Data Request Form, and Red Hat will ensure their personal data is properly processed and disposed of with parental consent.
What safeguards exist for international data transfers?

Where required by applicable law, Red Hat has put in place appropriate safeguards such as standard contractual clauses approved by the European Commission in accordance with applicable legal requirements to ensure data is adequately protected. Red Hat protects personal data as provided in the Privacy Statement and complies with applicable legal requirements.
What is the EU-U.S. Data Privacy Framework?

The EU-U.S. Data Privacy Framework is a mechanism for transferring personal data from the European Economic Area to the United States. Red Hat, Inc. and Red Hat Professional Consulting, Inc. have certified to this framework, providing a legal basis for international data transfers from the EEA to the U.S.
What is the Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Framework?

The Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Framework is a mechanism for transferring personal data from Switzerland to the United States. Red Hat, Inc. and Red Hat Professional Consulting, Inc. have certified to this framework, providing a legal basis for international data transfers from Switzerland to the U.S.
What is the UK Extension to the Data Privacy Framework?

The UK Extension is an extension to the EU-U.S. and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Frameworks for transferring personal data from the United Kingdom to the United States. Red Hat has certified to the UK Extension, providing a legal basis for international data transfers from the UK to the U.S.
How can I view Red Hat's Data Privacy Framework certification?

You can view Red Hat's Data Privacy Framework certification by visiting www.dataprivacyframework.gov/s/participant-search. Red Hat's certification demonstrates compliance with the EU-U.S., Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Frameworks, and UK Extension for international data transfers.
How often is Red Hat's privacy statement updated?

Red Hat reserves the right to update the Privacy Statement at any time. When changed in a material way, a notice is posted on the privacy statement page for 30 days, and the date of last update is indicated at the top of the statement. The current version was last updated October 13, 2025.
Can I access previous versions of Red Hat's privacy statement?

Yes, previous versions of the Privacy Statement are archived and available. You can request information related to previous versions or a copy of a previous version. The archive is available at redhat.com/en/about/privacy-policy/archive.
What happens if I don't refuse privacy statement changes?

If you do not refuse changes in writing within the 30-day notice period and continue using Red Hat websites, Red Hat considers that you have read and understand the Privacy Statement as changed, including with respect to personal data provided to Red Hat prior to the changes in the Privacy Statement.
What is Red Hat's EEA representative contact information?

Red Hat's representative contact information in the European Economic Area is: Red Hat Limited, 6700 Cork Airport Business Park, Phase II 1st Floor, Cork, Ireland. This is the contact point for EEA residents regarding privacy matters.
What is the Irish Data Protection Commissioner's contact information?

The Irish Data Protection Commissioner (Red Hat's lead supervisory authority) can be contacted at: Office of the Data Protection Commissioner, Canal House, Station Road, Portarlington, Co. Laois, R32 AP23, Ireland. Phone: +353 57 8684800 or +353 (0)761 104 800. Email: info@dataprotection.ie.
Can I get Red Hat's privacy statement in PDF format?

Yes, a PDF version of the Privacy Statement is available. You can download it from redhat.com/rhdc/managed-files/RH-Online-Privacy-Statement_English_13-Oct-2025.pdf.pdf. The PDF contains the same information as the online version.
What is Red Hat's U.S. toll-free number for privacy requests?

Red Hat's U.S. toll-free number for privacy requests, particularly for California consumers, is 1-800-546-7274. You can use this number to submit privacy requests or access information provided in the Privacy Statement in an alternative format.
What is the Personal Data Request Form?

The Personal Data Request Form is Red Hat's primary method for exercising privacy rights. It's available at redhat.com/en/about/personal-data-request and allows you to request information about data use, access data, rectify data, erase data, restrict use, object to processing, request data portability, withdraw consent, or submit appeals.
What is the California Supplemental Privacy Statement?

The California Supplemental Privacy Statement provides California-specific privacy disclosures required under California privacy laws. It includes information about sensitive personal information and 'sales' of personal data. California consumers should review this statement in addition to the main Privacy Statement.
What is the Red Hat Candidate Privacy Notice?

The Red Hat Candidate Privacy Notice provides detailed information about how Red Hat processes personal data during the recruitment, job application, and hiring process. Candidates are referred to this notice for information about candidate data processing. It's available at redhat.com/en/about/candidate-privacy-notice.
What is the Data Privacy Framework Notice?

The Data Privacy Framework Notice provides detailed information about Red Hat's certification to the EU-U.S. and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Frameworks and UK Extension. It explains how these frameworks provide a legal basis for international data transfers. It's available at redhat.com/en/about/dpf-notice.
Does Red Hat's privacy statement apply to open source projects?

No, this Privacy Statement may not apply to open source project websites sponsored by Red Hat. Such project websites may have their own privacy statements, which Red Hat encourages you to review. The main Privacy Statement applies to redhat.com and websites where Red Hat posts a direct link to this statement.
What are co-branded websites?

Co-branded websites are websites where Red Hat presents content together with one or more business partners. These websites may be governed by additional or different privacy statements. You should refer to the privacy statement on those specific websites for information about applicable privacy practices.
Does Red Hat's privacy statement apply to OpenShift Online?

Red Hat's obligations regarding personal data held on behalf of customers in cloud services like OpenShift Online are defined in agreements with customers and are not governed by this Privacy Statement. This statement applies to Red Hat's collection of data, not data that customers store using Red Hat cloud services.
What is Red Hat's legal basis for processing personal data?

Red Hat processes personal data based on various legal bases: contractual obligations (fulfilling requests, providing services), legitimate business interests (identifying users, providing product information, enhancing websites), consent (where required by law for marketing), and legal obligations (compliance with laws, responding to legal requests).
How does Red Hat use data for market research?

Red Hat or its representatives may contact you as part of customer satisfaction surveys or for market research purposes. This helps Red Hat understand customer needs, improve products and services, and measure satisfaction. Survey participation is typically voluntary, and you can opt out of marketing communications.
What data does Red Hat collect during event registration?

Red Hat collects information about your registration or participation in classes, exams, certifications, training, webcasts, or other events. This includes contact information, preferences, and information needed to facilitate your participation. This data is used to fulfill your event requests and provide event-related communications.
How does Red Hat use data for business intelligence?

Red Hat uses your personal data to develop internal marketing and business intelligence. This includes measuring the effectiveness of marketing campaigns and events, developing and modeling marketing audiences, assisting in prioritization, and tailoring communication efforts to better serve customers and prospects.
What payment information does Red Hat collect?

Red Hat collects payment information when you make purchases, which is used to process orders and fulfill contractual obligations. Payment information is encrypted during transmission. Red Hat may also collect credit and billing status information to manage your account and process transactions.
How does Red Hat use purchase records?

Red Hat uses purchase records to fulfill your orders, provide support for products you've obtained, understand your interests for marketing purposes, develop business intelligence, personalize your experience, and draw inferences about products you may be interested in based on your purchase history.
What product usage information does Red Hat collect?

Red Hat collects information about your use of products or services, which may include how you interact with products, features you use, errors you encounter, and performance data. This information helps Red Hat provide support, improve products, and understand how customers use Red Hat offerings.
How does Red Hat use support inquiry data?

Red Hat uses support inquiry data to provide technical support, answer questions, resolve issues, improve support services, and understand common problems or needs. Support inquiries may include contact information, account details, product information, and descriptions of issues or questions.
What preferences does Red Hat remember?

Red Hat remembers your preferred language and other preferences such as website settings, communication preferences, product interests, and personalization settings. These preferences are used to customize your experience, deliver relevant content, and tailor communications to your interests.
How does Red Hat use browsing history?

Red Hat uses browsing history to analyze website effectiveness, personalize your experience, understand your interests, measure marketing campaign effectiveness, improve website design and functionality, and deliver content and advertisements relevant to your browsing behavior.
What browser information does Red Hat collect?

Red Hat collects browser type and language information when you visit websites. This information helps Red Hat ensure websites function properly with your browser, provide appropriate language versions, troubleshoot technical issues, and optimize website performance for different browsers.
What operating system information does Red Hat collect?

Red Hat collects operating system information when you visit websites. This information helps Red Hat ensure compatibility, troubleshoot technical issues, optimize website performance for different operating systems, and understand the technical environment of website visitors.
How does Red Hat determine location from IP address?

Red Hat may determine your physical location based on your IP address when you visit websites. This location information is used for purposes like personalizing experience, providing relevant content, complying with regional requirements, and supporting business operations. Location may also be provided directly if you give your postal address.
What date and time information does Red Hat collect?

Red Hat collects date and time information when you visit websites, interact with emails, or use services. This information helps Red Hat understand usage patterns, analyze website traffic, measure campaign effectiveness, troubleshoot issues, and maintain security logs.
How does Red Hat use data for email personalization?

Red Hat uses your personal data to personalize email communications, deliver content and product offerings relevant to your interests, measure email effectiveness (open rates, click-through rates), and tailor communication efforts. This includes using web beacons to track email interactions.
What is Red Hat's policy on data minimization?

Red Hat collects personal data only to the extent necessary for the purposes described in the Privacy Statement. Red Hat does not use personal data in a manner inconsistent with the purpose of its original collection unless additional notice is provided and consent is obtained. Red Hat retains data only as long as required for processing purposes.
How does Red Hat ensure data accuracy?

Red Hat supplements data collected from you with additional data from third parties to help improve overall accuracy and completeness of information. Red Hat also allows you to access and modify your personal data online or through the Personal Data Request Form to ensure accuracy.
What happens if Red Hat receives personal data inadvertently?

If Red Hat inadvertently receives personal data, such as in telemetry data where personal information is not intended to be collected, Red Hat will delete it in accordance with the Privacy Statement. Red Hat states it does not intend to collect personal information through telemetry.
What is Red Hat's policy on automated decision-making?

Red Hat may use artificial intelligence and machine learning capabilities to analyze data, identify trends, make predictions, and provide AI-generated responses. However, the Privacy Statement does not specifically address automated decision-making. You may have rights regarding automated processing under applicable Data Protection Laws.
How does Red Hat use data for fraud prevention?

Red Hat uses personal data to verify your identity and ensure security of your personal data, which helps prevent fraud. Red Hat also uses data to authenticate users, maintain account security, and detect suspicious activity. This is part of Red Hat's security measures to protect personal data.
What is Red Hat's policy on profiling?

Red Hat draws inferences from personal data, such as what topics you may be interested in or what products you may be interested in, which may constitute profiling. Red Hat uses this for personalization and marketing. You may have rights regarding profiling under applicable Data Protection Laws, including the right to object.
How does Red Hat use data for single sign-on?

Red Hat uses required cookies to allow you to sign into different portions of Red Hat websites with the use of only one login. This single sign-on functionality improves user experience by eliminating the need to log in multiple times when accessing different sections of Red Hat websites.
What is Red Hat's policy on data portability?

You have the right to receive your personal data in a usable format and transmit it to a third party (data portability), subject to applicable Data Protection Laws. You can also request transfer of your personal data to a third party. Submit these requests via the Personal Data Request Form.
How does Red Hat handle data subject access requests?

Red Hat handles data subject access requests through the Personal Data Request Form. Before responding, Red Hat verifies the request by asking for at least two or three identifiers. Red Hat will provide information about data use, access to data, or take other requested actions in accordance with applicable Data Protection Laws.
What is Red Hat's response time for privacy requests?

The Privacy Statement does not specify exact response times, but Red Hat will respond to privacy requests in accordance with applicable Data Protection Laws, which typically require responses within 30-45 days depending on jurisdiction. Red Hat may request additional time if the request is complex.
How does Red Hat handle data breach notifications?

The Privacy Statement does not specifically address data breach notification procedures, but Red Hat implements security measures to protect personal data. In the event of a data breach, Red Hat would comply with applicable legal requirements for notification, which vary by jurisdiction and may include notifying affected individuals and data protection authorities.
What is Red Hat's policy on cross-border data transfers?

Red Hat is a global organization and may transfer personal data to Red Hat entities in the United States and elsewhere. Where required by applicable law, Red Hat has put in place appropriate safeguards such as standard contractual clauses or Data Privacy Framework certifications to ensure data is adequately protected during international transfers.
How does Red Hat use data for customer segmentation?

Red Hat uses personal data to develop and model marketing audiences, which involves customer segmentation based on interests, purchase history, website behavior, and other factors. This segmentation helps Red Hat tailor communications, prioritize outreach, and deliver relevant product and service information.
What is Red Hat's policy on data retention for legal claims?

Red Hat retains personal data for valid reasons including establishing, exercising, or defending legal claims. This means Red Hat may retain data beyond the period needed for original processing purposes if necessary for legal proceedings, dispute resolution, or protecting Red Hat's legal rights.
How does Red Hat use data for A/B testing?

Red Hat may use personal data, particularly internet activity and inferences, to test different website designs, measure website quality, and evaluate the effectiveness of online advertising. This helps Red Hat optimize website performance and improve user experience through data-driven design decisions.
What is Red Hat's policy on data sharing with government authorities?

Red Hat may disclose personal data as required by law or legal process, such as responding to duly authorized and lawful requests of police or public authorities, including to meet national security or law enforcement requirements. Red Hat will comply with such requests in accordance with applicable law.
How does Red Hat use data for product development?

Red Hat uses personal data including product usage information, support inquiries, website interactions, and feedback to help improve and further develop websites, products, and services. This includes understanding how products are used, identifying areas for improvement, and developing new features based on customer needs.
What is Red Hat's policy on data processing for PRC residents?

If you are in the People's Republic of China (PRC, excluding Hong Kong, Macau and Taiwan), the information necessary for entering into or performing a contract with Red Hat may be collected and processed without your express consent, or the act of providing the information to Red Hat is deemed as consent.
How does Red Hat use data for event management?

Red Hat uses personal data collected during event registration and participation to facilitate events, communicate event information, manage attendance, provide event materials, conduct surveys, and follow up after events. This includes information about registration, participation, preferences, and feedback.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for training and certification?

Red Hat uses personal data collected during training and certification registration and participation to manage courses, exams, and certifications, track progress, issue certificates, provide support, and communicate about training opportunities. This data is used to fulfill your training and certification requests.
How does Red Hat use data for newsletter subscriptions?

Red Hat uses your contact information and preferences to send newsletters and other materials you've subscribed to. You can opt out of marketing emails at any time by clicking unsubscribe links or contacting Red Hat. Newsletter subscriptions are subject to your preferences and applicable consent requirements.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for whitepapers and materials?

Red Hat uses contact information and preferences you provide when requesting whitepapers or other materials to fulfill your request, send the requested materials, and may use this information for marketing purposes subject to your preferences and applicable consent requirements.
How does Red Hat use data for promotions and contests?

Red Hat uses personal data collected during participation in promotions, contests, or giveaways to administer the promotion or contest, select winners, fulfill prizes, communicate about the promotion, and comply with legal requirements. Participation is voluntary and subject to terms and conditions of each promotion.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for chatbot interactions?

Red Hat collects personal data when you submit questions or comments via chatbots. This data is used to respond to your inquiries, provide support, improve chatbot functionality, and may be used for other purposes described in the Privacy Statement. Chatbot interactions may be logged for quality and improvement purposes.
How does Red Hat use data from customer portal pages?

Red Hat collects personal data when you submit content or posts on customer portal pages or other interactive webpages. This data is used to facilitate the interactive features, provide support, improve services, and may be used for other purposes described in the Privacy Statement. Remember that posts may become publicly available.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for call center interactions?

Personal data collected through call center interactions may be combined with data collected online. Red Hat uses this data to fulfill your requests, provide support, manage your account, and for other purposes described in the Privacy Statement. Call center interactions may be recorded for quality and training purposes.
How does Red Hat use data for email interaction tracking?

Red Hat uses web beacons and cookies to track your interactions with Red Hat emails, including open rates and click-through rates. This information helps Red Hat measure email effectiveness, understand engagement, improve email communications, and personalize future email content.
What is Red Hat's policy on hashed email addresses?

In select geographies, Red Hat may use activity linked to your hashed email address through various technologies. This allows Red Hat to analyze effectiveness of websites and marketing efforts, personalize experience, and better understand levels of interest and engagement while maintaining privacy through hashing.
How does Red Hat use data for third-party site tracking?

Red Hat may use remarketing technology to deliver targeted Red Hat advertisements on other websites you may visit. A third party may place or read a unique ad-serving cookie on your device and use technical information about your browser and activity at Red Hat websites to serve advertisements on non-Red Hat websites.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for traffic balancing?

Red Hat uses required cookies to balance traffic on websites, which helps ensure optimal website performance and availability. This technical function is necessary for website operation and does not involve marketing use of personal data.
How does Red Hat use data for form prepopulation?

Red Hat uses functional cookies to prepopulate forms you have already completed, which improves user experience by reducing the need to re-enter information. This convenience feature helps streamline interactions with Red Hat websites and services.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for error tracking?

Red Hat uses functional cookies that collect information about how you use websites, including whether you experience any errors. These cookies use aggregate information to provide statistics, help improve websites by measuring errors, and assist in troubleshooting technical issues.
How does Red Hat use data for shopping cart functionality?

Red Hat uses required cookies to remember items in your shopping cart, which is necessary for e-commerce functionality. This allows you to add items to your cart and complete purchases without losing cart contents when navigating the website.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for login session management?

Red Hat uses functional cookies to show you when you are logged into the system and allow you to remain logged-in for a period of time. This improves user experience by maintaining your authenticated session and reducing the need to repeatedly log in.
How does Red Hat use data for support services?

Red Hat uses functional cookies to help provide support services, which may include remembering your login information, tracking support interactions, and facilitating support requests. This helps Red Hat deliver efficient and personalized support experiences.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for survey invitations?

Red Hat uses functional cookies to invite you to participate in surveys or evaluations. This helps Red Hat gather feedback, measure satisfaction, conduct market research, and improve products and services based on customer input.
How does Red Hat use data for language preference management?

Red Hat uses functional cookies to remember your language and site preferences, which allows Red Hat to deliver content in your preferred language and customize your website experience according to your preferences. This improves usability and user satisfaction.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for website design testing?

Red Hat uses functional cookies that collect aggregate information to help test different designs on websites. This A/B testing helps Red Hat optimize website layout, functionality, and user experience based on how different designs perform with users.
How does Red Hat use data for advertising effectiveness measurement?

Red Hat uses functional cookies to measure the effectiveness of online advertising. This includes tracking how advertising campaigns perform, understanding which ads are most effective, and optimizing advertising strategies to improve return on investment and relevance to users.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for user interest understanding?

Red Hat uses functional cookies to understand what interests users, which helps Red Hat personalize content, tailor communications, develop marketing audiences, and deliver relevant product and service information. This improves the relevance of Red Hat's interactions with you.
How does Red Hat use data for website quality measurement?

Red Hat uses functional cookies to measure website quality, which includes tracking errors, performance metrics, user satisfaction indicators, and overall website effectiveness. This helps Red Hat identify areas for improvement and maintain high-quality website experiences.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for traffic optimization?

Red Hat uses functional cookies to measure traffic on websites so Red Hat can optimize content accordingly. This includes understanding which pages are most visited, how users navigate websites, and what content is most engaging, helping Red Hat prioritize and improve website content.
How does Red Hat use data for remarketing?

Red Hat uses remarketing technology to deliver targeted Red Hat advertisements on other websites you may visit. This involves using advertising cookies and third-party ad-serving technology to show you relevant Red Hat ads based on your previous interactions with Red Hat websites.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for ad relevance?

Red Hat uses advertising cookies to show you ads that are more relevant to you. Red Hat may disclose information to advertisers or use it to better understand your interests. Advertising cookies may be used to disclose data to advertisers so that ads you see are more relevant to your interests.
How does Red Hat handle data when I set Do Not Track?

If you have set your browser's global privacy settings such as selecting 'Do Not Track,' Red Hat recognizes these settings to signal your choice to opt-out of 'sales' and targeted advertising as appropriate under applicable Data Protection Laws. Red Hat respects your browser privacy preferences.
What happens if I reject all Red Hat cookies?

If you reject cookies, Required cookies will remain and cannot be turned off as they are necessary for website functionality. However, rejecting Functional and Advertising cookies may reduce the performance and functionality of Red Hat websites, as these cookies enable features like personalization and analytics.
How does Red Hat use data for security maintenance?

Red Hat uses required cookies to maintain and improve the safety and security of websites. This includes authenticating account users, allowing access to private accounts, detecting suspicious activity, and implementing security measures to protect personal data and website integrity.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for account authentication?

Red Hat uses required cookies to authenticate account users, which is necessary for allowing you to access your private accounts on websites. This authentication ensures that only authorized users can access account information and protected areas of Red Hat websites.
How does Red Hat use data for private account access?

Red Hat uses required cookies to allow you to access your private accounts on websites. This includes maintaining your authenticated session, verifying your identity, and ensuring that you can access account-specific information and features that require authentication.
What is Red Hat's policy on data retention for regulatory compliance?

Red Hat retains personal data for other valid reasons including compliance with legal and regulatory obligations. This means Red Hat may retain data longer than needed for original processing purposes if required by law, regulations, or to comply with legal obligations in various jurisdictions.
How does Red Hat use data for dispute resolution?

Red Hat retains personal data for valid reasons including dispute resolution. This means Red Hat may retain data if necessary to resolve disputes, address complaints, or handle legal matters. Data retention for dispute resolution helps protect both Red Hat and customers in case of disagreements.
What is Red Hat's policy on data retention for agreement enforcement?

Red Hat retains personal data for valid reasons including enforcing agreements. This means Red Hat may retain data if necessary to enforce terms of service, license agreements, or other contracts. This helps ensure compliance with agreements and protects Red Hat's contractual rights.
How does Red Hat use data for legal claim defense?

Red Hat retains personal data for valid reasons including the establishment, exercise, or defense of legal claims. This means Red Hat may retain data if necessary for legal proceedings, defending against claims, or pursuing legal rights. This is a standard practice for protecting legal interests.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for identity verification?

Red Hat uses personal data to verify your identity when you exercise privacy rights or access services. This includes asking for identifiers like name, email address, and location. Additional verification may be requested depending on your relationship with Red Hat and the nature of the request.
How does Red Hat use data for authorized agent verification?

When an authorized agent submits a privacy request, Red Hat asks the agent to provide name, email address, description of relationship with the individual, and to certify that the representative has permission to submit the request. Red Hat may also request proof of written permission or Power of Attorney.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for appeal processing?

If Red Hat denies a privacy rights request and applicable Data Protection Laws provide a right to appeal, you may appeal the decision. Submit appeals by selecting 'Other' via the Personal Data Request Form or by emailing privacy@redhat.com. Red Hat will process appeals in accordance with applicable legal requirements.
How does Red Hat use data for consent withdrawal?

You can withdraw consent previously given for data processing by submitting withdrawal requests via the Personal Data Request Form. However, withdrawal of consent does not affect the lawfulness of processing based on consent before its withdrawal. Red Hat will stop processing based on consent after withdrawal.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for marketing opt-out?

You can opt out of receiving marketing emails from Red Hat at any time without cost by clicking unsubscribe links in marketing emails or contacting Red Hat via the Personal Data Request Form. You can also set preferences when creating an account or registering for services to control marketing communications.
How does Red Hat use data for non-discrimination compliance?

When you exercise privacy rights under applicable Data Protection Laws, you have a right not to receive discriminatory treatment from Red Hat. Red Hat will not penalize or treat you differently for requesting information, accessing data, or exercising other privacy rights. This is a legal requirement in many jurisdictions.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for alternative format requests?

California consumers can request access to information provided in the Privacy Statement in an alternative format by calling Red Hat's U.S. toll-free number at 1-800-546-7274. This accommodates different accessibility needs and ensures information is available in formats that are usable for all consumers.
How does Red Hat use data for email encryption?

Red Hat encrypts certain personal data like payment information when transmitting such information over the Internet. This encryption helps protect sensitive data during transmission and is part of Red Hat's technical safeguards to protect personal data from unauthorized access, use, and disclosure.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for partner data protection?

Red Hat requires that business partners and service providers protect information from unauthorized access, use, and disclosure through contractual obligations. Partners are required by contract to keep information received on behalf of Red Hat confidential and secure and not use it for any purpose other than the purpose provided.
How does Red Hat use data for physical security measures?

Red Hat implements appropriate physical safeguards to help protect personal data from unauthorized access, use, and disclosure. While the Privacy Statement doesn't detail specific physical security measures, physical safeguards are part of Red Hat's comprehensive security approach to protect personal data.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for administrative security?

Red Hat implements appropriate administrative safeguards to help protect personal data from unauthorized access, use, and disclosure. Administrative safeguards include policies, procedures, and controls that govern how Red Hat handles personal data and ensures appropriate access controls and data handling practices.
How does Red Hat use data for technical security measures?

Red Hat implements appropriate technical safeguards to help protect personal data from unauthorized access, use, and disclosure. This includes encryption of certain data during transmission, secure authentication, access controls, and other technical measures to protect data integrity and confidentiality.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for social network integration?

Red Hat provides social media features that enable sharing information with social networks and interacting with Red Hat on social media websites. Your use of these features may result in collection or sharing of information about you. Red Hat encourages you to review privacy policies and settings on social media websites.
How does Red Hat use data for public forum moderation?

Red Hat websites may make chat rooms, forums, blogs, message boards, and news groups available. Red Hat may use data from these public forums for moderation, improving services, and understanding user needs. Remember that your comments and posts become publicly available, so exercise discretion when submitting content.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for linked website tracking?

Red Hat does not control and is not responsible for information collected by websites reached through links from Red Hat websites. If you have questions about data collection procedures of linked websites, contact those organizations directly. Red Hat's Privacy Statement does not apply to third-party linked websites.
How does Red Hat use data for parental consent processing?

If you are a parent or guardian of a minor under 16 and believe they have disclosed personal data to Red Hat, contact Red Hat via the Personal Data Request Form. Red Hat will ensure their personal data is properly processed and disposed of with the consent of their parent or guardian, in accordance with applicable law.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for standard contractual clauses?

Where required by applicable law, Red Hat has put in place appropriate safeguards such as standard contractual clauses approved by the European Commission in accordance with applicable legal requirements to ensure data is adequately protected during international transfers. These clauses provide legal protection for cross-border data transfers.
How does Red Hat use data for framework compliance verification?

Red Hat's certification to the EU-U.S. and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Frameworks and UK Extension demonstrates compliance with international data transfer requirements. You can verify Red Hat's certification by visiting www.dataprivacyframework.gov/s/participant-search to view the certification status.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for framework information?

To learn more about the EU-U.S. and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Frameworks and UK Extension, visit www.dataprivacyframework.gov/s/. Red Hat's Data Privacy Framework Notice at redhat.com/en/about/dpf-notice provides detailed information about Red Hat's certification and how these frameworks apply to data transfers.
How does Red Hat use data for material change notifications?

When Red Hat makes material changes to the Privacy Statement, a notice is posted on the privacy statement page for 30 days, and the date of last update is indicated at the top. If you do not refuse changes in writing within that period and continue using websites, Red Hat considers that you have read and understand the Privacy Statement as changed.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for statement archiving?

Previous versions of the Privacy Statement are archived and available. You can request information related to previous versions or a copy of a previous version. The archive is available at redhat.com/en/about/privacy-policy/archive, allowing you to review how the Privacy Statement has evolved over time.
How does Red Hat use data for periodic statement review?

Red Hat encourages you to periodically review the Privacy Statement for any changes or updates. The Privacy Statement may be updated at any time, and material changes will be posted with a 30-day notice. Regular review helps ensure you understand how Red Hat handles your personal data.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for EEA authority contact?

If you are a resident of the European Economic Area and wish to raise a concern about Red Hat's use of your personal data, you may direct questions or complaints to the Irish Data Protection Commissioner (Red Hat's lead supervisory authority) or contact your local supervisory authority. Contact information is provided in the Privacy Statement.
How does Red Hat use data for PDF statement distribution?

Red Hat provides a PDF version of the Privacy Statement for convenience and accessibility. The PDF is available at redhat.com/rhdc/managed-files/RH-Online-Privacy-Statement_English_13-Oct-2025.pdf.pdf and contains the same information as the online version, allowing you to download and review the statement offline.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for toll-free request handling?

For U.S. consumers, particularly California consumers, you may submit privacy requests by calling Red Hat's U.S. toll-free number at 1-800-546-7274. You can also access information provided in the Privacy Statement in an alternative format by calling this number, accommodating different communication preferences.
How does Red Hat use data for email privacy inquiries?

You can contact Red Hat about privacy practices or use of your personal data by emailing privacy@redhat.com. This email address is monitored for privacy inquiries, requests, and concerns. Red Hat will respond to email inquiries in accordance with applicable Data Protection Laws and Red Hat's privacy practices.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for mail privacy inquiries?

You can contact Red Hat about privacy by mail at Red Hat, Inc., Corporate Legal Group, 100 East Davie Street, Raleigh, North Carolina 27601, United States. For EEA inquiries, contact Red Hat Limited, 6700 Cork Airport Business Park, Phase II 1st Floor, Cork, Ireland. Mail inquiries will be processed in accordance with applicable law.
How does Red Hat use data for form-based request processing?

Red Hat processes privacy requests primarily through the Personal Data Request Form at redhat.com/en/about/personal-data-request. This form allows you to submit various types of privacy requests including access, rectification, erasure, restriction, object, portability, consent withdrawal, and appeals. The form streamlines request processing.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for California-specific disclosures?

California consumers should see the California Supplemental Privacy Statement at redhat.com/en/about/california-supplemental-privacy-statement to review California-required disclosures on sensitive personal information and 'sales' of personal data. This provides California-specific privacy information in addition to the main Privacy Statement.
How does Red Hat use data for candidate privacy compliance?

Red Hat processes candidate data in accordance with the Red Hat Candidate Privacy Notice at redhat.com/en/about/candidate-privacy-notice. This notice provides detailed information about how Red Hat processes personal data during recruitment, job application, and hiring processes, which is separate from the main Privacy Statement.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for framework notice compliance?

Red Hat provides a Data Privacy Framework Notice at redhat.com/en/about/dpf-notice that explains Red Hat's certification to the EU-U.S. and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Frameworks and UK Extension. This notice provides detailed information about how these frameworks apply to international data transfers.
How does Red Hat use data for open source project privacy?

This Privacy Statement may not apply to open source project websites sponsored by Red Hat. Such project websites may have their own privacy statements, which Red Hat encourages you to review. The main Privacy Statement applies to redhat.com and websites where Red Hat posts a direct link to this statement.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for co-branded website privacy?

Co-branded websites where Red Hat presents content together with business partners may be governed by additional or different privacy statements. You should refer to the privacy statement on those specific websites for information about applicable privacy practices, as they may differ from Red Hat's main Privacy Statement.
How does Red Hat use data for cloud service customer data?

Red Hat's obligations regarding personal data held on behalf of customers in cloud services like OpenShift Online are defined in agreements with customers and are not governed by this Privacy Statement. This statement applies to Red Hat's collection of data, not data that customers store using Red Hat cloud services.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for contract performance?

Red Hat processes personal data to comply with contractual obligations to you, such as when responding to your requests, providing products and services, fulfilling orders, providing support, and managing your account. This is a legal basis for processing under applicable Data Protection Laws.
How does Red Hat use data for legitimate business interests?

Red Hat processes personal data based on legitimate business interests, such as identifying and authenticating users, providing information about products and services, enhancing website experience, improving products and services, and supporting marketing and sales efforts. This is a legal basis for processing under applicable Data Protection Laws.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for consent-based processing?

Where required by applicable law, Red Hat will obtain your consent before sending marketing messages or processing certain types of data. You can withdraw consent at any time via the Personal Data Request Form. Withdrawal does not affect the lawfulness of processing based on consent before withdrawal.
How does Red Hat use data for legal obligation compliance?

Red Hat processes personal data to comply with legal and regulatory obligations, such as responding to lawful requests from authorities, meeting national security or law enforcement requirements, complying with data protection laws, and fulfilling legal requirements in various jurisdictions where Red Hat operates.
What is Red Hat's policy on data use for purpose limitation?

Red Hat will not use your personal data in a manner inconsistent with the purpose of its original collection, unless Red Hat has provided you additional notice and you have consented. This purpose limitation principle ensures that data is used only for the purposes for which it was originally collected.
How does Red Hat use data for retention limitation?
